title: Executive Order 11623—Delegating to the Director of Selective Service Authority to Issue Rules and Regulations Under the Military Selective Service Act

flags.justification: Sec. 2 requires the Director to seek the views of seven named officials before any rule takes effect, with a real right for any of them to demand presidential review upon disagreement — a genuinely checked, multi-layered delegation.
Mandatory Federal Register publication, a defined consultation period, and a required certification of compliance make this an extremely well-specified, transparent process.
A rule-making process delegation, not itself a compliance regime.
No compliance regime exists for an incentive structure to attach to.
A standing regulatory-authority delegation for an ongoing statutory program.
Sec. 3 explicitly preserves the President's own authority to waive requirements or issue rules directly — a non-exclusive delegation that does not lock out presidential involvement.
The seven-way consultation-and-objection process is a genuinely robust internal check, even though no formal third-party compliance regime exists.
This delegates what was previously presidential rule-making authority over the draft system, a matter of major significance during the Vietnam War era, though the delegation is carefully checked.
An elaborate, deliberate multi-agency consultation structure protective of other agencies' interests in draft-related rules.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 1/12 = 8.3%. One of the most elaborately checked delegation designs in this corpus: a seven-way multi-agency consultation-and-escalation-to-the-President process for draft-system rule-making, issued during the Vietnam War era when such rules carried genuine, contested significance.
